narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strength and determination it takes to get here. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.60, an LSAT score of 34, Year 1 school decile ranking of 3rd out of 10, Year 3 decile of 2nd out of 10, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time—details that paint a picture of your unique path. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 58% chance you'll face failure on that crucial test, tipping the scales just enough to reject your application for now. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged most heavily on your Year 3 decile ranking, which stood out as lower than typical (around 5th or 6th for most students) and strongly nudged the model toward foreseeing a bar struggle. Your LSAT score, below the average of about 37, also pulled in the opposite direction by suggesting potential challenges, though less forcefully than your later academic decile. Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA, similarly under the usual 3.2 mark, added some counterbalance toward passing but couldn't outweigh the top concerns. Your upper-middle family income worked against you slightly compared to broader averages, while your Year 1 decile and full-time plans offered minor boosts aligning with successful peers. Overall, your later academic performance loomed largest, setting you apart from students whose stronger deciles correlated with bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many in similar positions, especially women of color, have rewritten their stories through targeted growth. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to aim higher, bolstering your GPA with post-bac courses or explaining your decile dips in a personal statement, and exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your background. You're resilient, and with focused steps, doors will open wider. Keep pushing forward.
